text: The surgeon demonstrates the placement of ports for a left-sided pulmonary lobectomy. The sub-xiphoid incision is marked from the umbilicus to the xiphoid process, extending inferiorly. The ninth rib is marked posteriorly, with ports placed above and below it. The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position, with drapes placed low for access. The surgeon makes a vertical incision along the linea alba, inserting a finger behind the sternum to guide the insertion of instruments under vision.

Now I'm going to show you the whole robotic lobectomy of this patient. It was a particularly difficult one with adhesions, and I spent quite a lot of time getting adhesions down before positioning the ports. The surgeon's assistant passes rolled-up swabs through the subxiphoid port, which is visible under direct vision. The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using VATS techniques in preparation for the right lower lobectomy, with the port positioned posteriorly.

The robotic arm number two holds the lower lobe while the surgeon uses a Maryland bipolar dissector to divide adhesions between the lower and middle lobes, as well as to the diaphragm and chest wall. The surgeon employs Bob Sofoglio's setup for focused energy application, ensuring a dry dissection. Arm two is equipped with a cadier, and arm three with a lung grasper, which fits through a five-millimeter port, facilitating the dissection process.

The surgeon begins dissection around the basal pulmonary artery using a Maryland dissector, while the assistant uses a suction device to aid in the process. The sub-xiphoid port provides a clear view for firing along the fissure, facilitating the removal of a station eleven lymph node through a twelve mil port.

The surgeon navigates around a large nest of R-Eleven lymph nodes near the pulmonary artery, using a lymph node grasper to remove them through the port without losing any tissue. The Maryland dissector is positioned over the apical segmental pulmonary artery, while a purple 45 stapler is used to clear the oblique fissure for better visualization of the pulmonary artery. The lung remains adherent to the diaphragm and posterior chest wall. The surgeon plans to address the artery, bronchus, and vein sequentially, utilizing the robotic arms to lift and guide the stapler, ensuring precise direction and protection of the pulmonary artery during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a lung grasper to push down on the lower lobe, identifying the apical segmental vessels. A tan tri-stapler is used to staple the first apical segmental vessel, followed by the discovery and stapling of a second apical segmental vessel. The surgeon notes the use of CO₂ in the chest to push the diaphragm down, facilitating access above the diaphragm. The pulmonary artery and apical segmental vessels are completed, and the surgeon proceeds to dissect the posterior oblique fissure, using Maryland forceps to reach the exit point and retract the upper lobe forward to visualize the azygos vein.

The surgeon visualizes the middle lobe bronchus and cleans the bronchus intermedius and lower lobe bronchus. Using a Maryland dissector and cadier, the surgeon brushes away tumor tissue from the chest wall. The surgeon removes additional lymph nodes from the hilum and clears the space below the bronchus, revealing the lower lobe vein. The middle lobe bronchus is visible to the right, and the apical segmental branch to the lower lobe is identified. A yellow celastic sling is used, and the stapler is advanced at a perfect angle through the subxiphoid port. The assistant pushes down on the lower lobe to ensure a proper curve without stenosing the middle lobe. The artery and bronchus are completed, leaving the vein as the last structure to be addressed.

The surgeon uses a sub-xiphoid approach to fire on the vein, completing the sequence of artery, bronchus, and vein. The lower lobe is held in a stable position as adhesiolysis is performed using a sucker and hook diathermy to remove adhesions from the diaphragm. The sample is placed in a bag and extracted through a pre-marked incision from the xiphisternum to the umbilicus, avoiding the rectus abdominis for a pain-free procedure. The lower lobe is removed without rib spreading.
